sections.paragraph:
Koph, the nineteenth Hebrew letter; when it stands as a numeral, i.q. a hundred. The name קֹוף, Arab. قُفُّ denotes the hole of an axe, and this agrees well with the form of this letter in the Phœnician and Hebrew alphabets. Its pronunciation differs from כ whether with or without dagesh, in that the sound of ק is produced from the back part of the palate near the throat with more effort; similarly to ט, see page 316 .

Koph is interchanged with the other palatals ג, כ, as has been shewn above (pp. 150, A , and 378, A ); it is also found to pass over to the gutturals, so that the roots קָטַר, עָטַר No. 1, to burn incense, are kindred. Also in the primary elements at least of the language, k appears also to have changed to t (just as children beginning to talk often substitute t for k as being easier of pronunciation); and thus kindred to one another are פָּקַח and פָּתָח to open, שָׁקָה and שָׁתָה to drink, פָּתָר Ethiop. פכר to interpret, אַתָּה and the lost אַכָּה (whence the suff. ךָ) thou; comp. κόπτω and τύπτω; quattuor and τέτταρες.
